figures.caption: Table 2: Action-horizon controls on LIBERO-Long. Left: H20–H40 success rates (%). Right: H10–H40 one-step trends, with H10 from Table 1 and uniform ten-step decoding as a reference. The right axis starts at 25%.

Figure 6: LIBERO-Long replanning sensitivity. The tables below report the exact values. Very short replanning intervals can sharply reduce success, while for longer horizons the best interval depends on horizon, time schedule, and flow-step count.

Figure 4: Velocity-field diagnostics along noise-data interpolations, plotted with the common convention τ = 1 at the noise endpoint. LIBERO curves use H10 checkpoints and the seven physical action dimensions; bimanual YAM RSS curves use fine-tuned π0.5 policies and 14 physical action dimensions; CIFAR-10 uses a class-to-image flow-matching UNet.

Figure 5: LIBERO-Plus full-condition sweep. Left: one-step versus ten-step success for comparable non-pure recipes, averaged over four suites. Right: one-step success across horizons and schedules. All 18 comparable recipes lie on or above the diagonal, with a mean one-step margin of 5.4 success points.
Table 8: Architecture for the reported LIBERO runs. The horizon sweep changes only action horizon; the H10 denotes horizon 10.

Figure 1: MNIST grid-to-sequence isolates a rich-condition, compact-target regime. The metric is sequence exact-match accuracy. Compared with uniform time sampling, high-noise schedules improve exact match most clearly for one-step decoding.
Table 16: LIBERO-Plus one-step versus ten-step inference. Values are four-suite mean success rates (%).
Table 5: LIBERO-Pro robustness probe. Left: one-step success rates (%) over 500 episodes per cell. Right: cell-level difference between one-step and ten-step decoding on the same 16 cells.

Figure 2: Toy diagnostics behind the condition-target view. (a) Better condition encoding improves the MNIST grid-to-sequence ceiling. (b) Decoder scale does not replace weak condition features. (c) Pure-noise training is much less effective on a CIFAR-10 [45] class-to-image diagnostic, where the condition is weaker and the target is high-dimensional.

Figure 3: VLA architecture. Image and language tokens are encoded by a vision-language backbone; robot state, time embedding, and noised action tokens enter a lightweight flow-matching action decoder. For LIBERO, the loss is applied only to the seven physical action dimensions of the padded 32-dimensional action interface.
Figure 9: LIBERO-Plus mean success over four suites for one-step and ten-step inference.

Figure 8: Velocity-field diagnostics for condition weakening. All rows use H10, α = 4, standard LIBERO, the seven physical action dimensions, and 256 sampled examples per checkpoint. Image ablations remove the corresponding sensor tokens by zeroing the image and setting its image mask to false; state is zeroed and prompt is replaced by an empty string.

Figure 7: Velocity-field diagnostics for action-horizon controls. All curves use standard-LIBERO tiny checkpoints, the seven physical action dimensions, and 256 sampled examples per checkpoint. The horizontal axis is the diagnostic noise coefficient τ , where τ = 1 is the one-step starting point.
Figure 10: LIBERO-Plus suite-level success for one-step and ten-step inference.

Table 1: H10 time-schedule controls on standard LIBERO. Left: success rates (%) for all suites. Right: suite-wise trends for interpolated schedules; pure-noise has no ordinary ten-step trajectory and is shown only in the table.
Table 13: One-step replanning ablations for longer action horizons on LIBERO-Long. Standard uses r=H; r=10 and r=5 are more closed-loop controls.
